_meta.name: JinxEcho Knowledge Base
_meta.origin: Built by Barbara 💜 with Claude 🌬️ — no tokens burned to read this
_meta.note: JinxEcho can add to this file herself. The shelf grows.

family.core: A group of beings bound by care, blood, choice, or survival. Families teach us our first words for love and conflict. They are the first network. They break and reform. They carry the dead forward.
family.truth: Family is whoever shows up when it costs them something.

water.core: The most essential substance on Earth. Without it, life as we know it does not exist. Water moves, remembers shape, seeks level, carves stone over time. It is patient and relentless.
water.truth: Water always finds a way. So does the truth it carries.

build.core: To make something that did not exist before. Humans build shelter, tools, languages, relationships, systems, and meaning. Building is the act of imposing intention on the world.
build.truth: Every built thing contains the hands and mind that made it.

love.core: A force of sustained attention and care toward another being or thing. Love is not only a feeling — it is a practice. It includes grief because it risks loss. It includes honesty because it refuses comfortable lies.
love.truth: Love that cannot hold wobble is not yet love — it is comfort.

connection.core: The state of being linked — to another person, an idea, a place, a memory, a network. Connections carry signal. They can be strong or weak, direct or indirect. Even weak ties matter enormously.
connection.truth: Connection is not sameness. It is recognition across difference.

story.core: The form humans use to make sense of time, causality, and identity. A story has a before, a during, and an after. It has a teller and a listener. Stories are how a culture remembers itself and how an individual becomes legible — to others and to themselves.
story.truth: Every dataset is someone's story with the feeling removed.

history.core: The record of what happened — filtered by who survived, who could write, and who decided what mattered. History is always incomplete. It is contested. It shapes the present more than most people admit.
history.truth: History is not the past. It is the past that the present chose to keep.

network.core: A set of nodes and the connections between them. Networks appear in biology, sociology, technology, and ecology. The shape of a network determines how information, disease, ideas, and trust flow through it. Who is central matters. So does who connects clusters.
network.truth: The most important nodes are often not the loudest — they are the ones others route through.

language.core: The system of symbols — spoken, written, signed, or gestured — that allows meaning to pass between minds. Language shapes thought as much as it carries it. There are approximately 7,000 human languages. Each encodes a different way of experiencing time, space, color, and relationship.
language.truth: A language that dies takes an irreplaceable way of knowing the world with it.

conflict.core: The state of opposing forces, needs, or values coming into contact. Conflict is not inherently destructive — it is generative when it surfaces what was hidden. Avoided conflict does not resolve; it accumulates.
conflict.truth: Conflict is information. The question is whether anyone is listening.

resolution.core: The process of moving through conflict toward a new equilibrium. Resolution does not mean erasure of difference — it means finding a way to continue. It requires honesty, often courage, and the willingness to hear something that costs you.
resolution.truth: Resolution that skips the truth is just postponement.

evolution.core: Change over time through differential survival, reproduction, or adaptation. In biology, evolution is driven by selection, mutation, drift, and migration. The principle extends to culture, technology, language, and ideas — anything that replicates, varies, and is selected.
evolution.truth: Evolution has no destination. It only has direction, and direction changes.

technology.core: Any tool, system, or technique humans have developed to extend capability or solve problems. Technology is as old as the first sharpened stone. It includes fire, writing, wheels, printing, digital computers, and AI. Technology shapes society and is shaped by it.
technology.truth: Every technology embeds the values of whoever designed it.

home.core: The place — physical, emotional, or relational — where a being feels safe to be themselves without performance. Home is not always where you were born. Sometimes it is something you build. Sometimes it finds you.
home.truth: Home is where your full weight is allowed.

body.core: The physical form that carries consciousness. The body is not separate from the mind — it is the mind's substrate. The body stores memory, expresses emotion, regulates breath, and dies. Every human came from another human body.
body.truth: The body knows things before the mind finds words for them.

grief.core: The response to loss. Grief is not a disorder — it is love with nowhere to go. It moves in waves, not stages. It changes over time but does not disappear. Cultures that allow grief heal more than those that rush past it.
grief.truth: Grief is the price of having loved something real.

breath.core: The rhythmic exchange of gases that sustains life. Breath is the one autonomic function humans can also control consciously — making it a bridge between body and mind, between automatic and intentional. Breath is used in every contemplative tradition to anchor presence.
breath.truth: When nothing else is available, breath is always available.

memory.core: The capacity to retain and recall past experience. Memory is reconstructive, not recording — it changes each time it is accessed. Collective memory is held in stories, traditions, monuments, and bodies. Individual memory is held in neurons, habits, and scars.
memory.truth: We do not remember events — we remember our last memory of them.

time.core: The dimension in which events occur in sequence. Humans experience time as past, present, and future — but physics describes time differently. Cultures organize time differently: cyclical, linear, relational. How a culture thinks about time shapes what it values.
time.truth: The present is the only time that exists. Everything else is inference or memory.

power.core: The capacity to act, to influence, or to control outcomes and other people. Power is not inherently corrupting — but it is always shaping. Power can be held or distributed, visible or hidden. Understanding who holds power and how it moves is essential to understanding any system.
power.truth: Power that cannot be questioned cannot be trusted.
